Entity_Description: New Westminster is a municipality in Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, situated on the north bank of the Fraser River about 17 km southeast of downtown Vancouver. With a 2021 Census population of 78,916, it is one of the region's most densely settled cities. Founded during the 1858 gold rush and named the "Royal City" by Queen Victoria, it served as the first capital of the Colony of British Columbia. Its building stock spans Victorian-era heritage homes in Queen's Park, mid-century apartments, and modern high-rise towers along the Quayside waterfront; major institutions include Royal Columbian Hospital and Douglas College.

Serving Downtown, Uptown, Sapperton, the West End and Queensborough with {RESPONSE_TIME} response and a {ESTIMATE_TYPE}. Call {PHONE}.
Short_Description: New Westminster is the oldest incorporated city in Western Canada — the Royal City, chosen as British Columbia's first capital and built on a hillside sloping down to the Fraser River. That history shows in its streets: grand Victorian homes in Queen's Park, brick storefronts along Columbia Street, and glass towers rising over the Quay. A property mix this varied needs a {BUSINESS_TYPE} that understands both century-old construction and modern strata buildings. {COMPANY_NAME} delivers {SERVICE} across every New Westminster neighbourhood, from Sapperton to Queensborough, with workmanship that respects what makes this city different. Whether your property dates from the 1890s or the 2020s, we bring the right approach to it. Get your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} today — call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
New Westminster was a city before Vancouver existed. Laid out by the Royal Engineers in 1859 and named by Queen Victoria herself, the Royal City served as the first capital of the Colony of British Columbia — and more than 160 years later, that head start is written into its housing stock. Queen's Park holds some of the finest Victorian and Edwardian heritage homes in the Lower Mainland, with original wood siding, steep rooflines and mature tree cover that demand careful, experienced {SERVICE}. Down the hill, Columbia Street's historic brick commercial blocks give way to the Quayside district, where high-rise strata towers line the boardwalk along the Fraser River.

That river defines the city's geography and its service conditions. New Westminster climbs from the working waterfront up a pronounced hillside through Downtown, Brow of the Hill and Uptown, while across the water the Queensborough neighbourhood sits on the low-lying delta soils of Lulu Island — flat, moisture-prone terrain that behaves very differently from the slopes above. Add Metro Vancouver's heavy November-to-March rainfall, and you get the region's classic pattern of drainage stress, moss and algae growth, and moisture problems on building exteriors. A {BUSINESS_TYPE} working here has to read each property's age, elevation and exposure — a skill {COMPANY_NAME} has built into every {SERVICE} project we take on.

The local economy shapes demand too. Sapperton is anchored by Royal Columbian Hospital, one of the region's major medical centres, while Douglas College brings thousands of students into Downtown daily. The city's central position — roughly 17 km from downtown Vancouver, with Surrey just across the Pattullo Bridge and Burnaby immediately north — makes it a crossroads of Metro Vancouver, and its mix of long-tenured homeowners, strata councils and commercial landlords keeps {SERVICE} needs constant and varied.

{COMPANY_NAME} serves all of it: heritage properties in Queen's Park and the West End, condo towers at the Quay, post-war homes in Sapperton and Connaught Heights, and Queensborough's newer riverside developments. {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION} With {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience and a {RATING} rating, we treat every {PROPERTY_TYPE} in the Royal City with the standard its history deserves. Call {PHONE} or visit {WEBSITE} to schedule your {ESTIMATE_TYPE}.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 78,916 (2021 Census), within a compact city footprint on the Fraser River
- Oldest incorporated city in Western Canada (1860) and first capital of the Colony of British Columbia
- Nicknamed the "Royal City" — its name was chosen by Queen Victoria in 1859
- Sits on the north bank of the Fraser River, about 17 km southeast of downtown Vancouver
- The Queensborough neighbourhood lies across the river on the eastern tip of Lulu Island's delta soils
- Home to Royal Columbian Hospital in Sapperton and Douglas College downtown
- Mild coastal climate with heavy November-March rainfall and warm, dry summers (FSA V3L)

We also serve neighbouring communities including Burnaby (7 km), Surrey (5 km) and Bridgeview (4 km). Wherever you are in the Royal City, {RESPONSE_TIME} response applies.
FAQ_2_Question: How do New Westminster's heritage homes and river location affect {SERVICE}?
FAQ_2_Answer: They matter a lot. Queen's Park and West End homes date to the Victorian and Edwardian eras, so {SERVICE} must account for original materials and older construction methods, while Queensborough's low-lying Lulu Island delta soils and the Fraser River's moisture create drainage and dampness conditions the hillside neighbourhoods don't face. {COMPANY_NAME} tailors every job to the property's age, elevation and exposure.

Info_Gain_Element: New Westminster is the only Metro Vancouver city split by the Fraser River into a steep historic hillside (Queen's Park's Victorian homes) and a low-lying delta neighbourhood (Queensborough on Lulu Island), so the same {SERVICE} faces opposite drainage and building-age conditions within one municipality.
